content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_optandum_1"> <gloss>what was chiefly to be wished:</gloss> not implying a protasis contrary to fact. See § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304, 3; G. 254, R.1; H. 583 (511, 1, N.8); H.-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_pertinebat_1"> <gloss>the one thing which most tended</gloss> (or, <gloss>was of chief importance).</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiam_infamiamque_1"> <gloss>odium and ill repute</gloss>, from the partisan use of the courts by the Senators. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vestri_ordinis_1">, i.e. the senatorial order. The word <foreign xml:lang="lat">ordo</foreign> signified, loosely, any recognized body of citizens—as freedmen, publicans, clerks; but it was more especially used of the two powerful classes of the Roman aristocracy, the Senatorial and the Equestrian, which struggled with each other for power during the last century of the Republic. The Senators, from whom the jurors were at this time taken (see note on Rosc. Am., p. 2,1. l), formed a limited (300 to 600) order of nobility which virtually controlled the government. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> constituted a moneyed aristocracy. Naturally these two orders had opposing interests, as the Senators were excluded from trade and the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> practically from political power. Their antagonism showed itself more especially in the matter of the provinces, which the Senators wished to oppress by official plunder and the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> by commercial extortion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summo_tempore_1">, most <gloss>critical time</gloss> (more lit. <gloss>extreme</gloss> crisis): the year of the consulship of Pompey and Crassus (<date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inveteravit_1"> (emphatic position), <gloss>there has come to be deeply rooted</gloss> (observe that the figure is quite different in the Latin). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opinio_1"> , <gloss> notion</gloss> or <gloss>idea</gloss> (not so strong as our <gloss> opinion</gloss>, which should be <foreign xml:lang="lat">sententia</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exteras_nationes_1">the reference is, of course, to the peoples subject to Rome, who were aggrieved by the rapacity of the provincial governors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_iudiciis_1">: in consequence of the situation described above (note on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ordinis_1">, l. 2) it became all important for one class or the other to control the courts, before which any misdoings of either party were likely to come for trial. For years these two orders had struggled for such control. At this particular time the courts were in the hands of the Senators, who were bound together by a common cause to shield any one of their number who might be charged with misconduct as a provincial governor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neminem_1"> (more emphatic than <foreign xml:lang="lat">nullum</foreign>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_1"> translate, <gloss>never.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_2"> (causal) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sint_2"> , <gloss>when men are ready.—</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contionibus_legibus_2"> <gloss>harangues and bills</gloss> (proposed laws). The proposition of a law which took the exclusive control of the courts from the Senators was even now pending, and the law (<foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Aurelia</foreign>) was passed before the case of Verres was decided. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conentur_2">, purpose clause. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magnitudine_2">, abl. of means. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spe_2">, abl. of specification. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="actor_2"> , <gloss> complainant</gloss>, i.e. agent or <gloss>attorney</gloss> for conducting the suit in personal processes (<gloss>in personam</gloss> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adduxi_hominem_2">etc.: cf. Burke, Impeachment of Warren Hastings: <q rend="double">We have brought before your Lordships the first man in property and power; we have brought before you the head, the chief, the captain-general in iniquity,—one in whom all the frauds, all the peculations, all the tyranny in India are embodied, disciplined, and arrayed. Then, if we have brought before you such a person, if you strike at him, you will not have need of a great many more examples,— you strike at the whole corps if you strike at the head:</q> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_quo_2"> , <gloss>in whose case</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reconciliare_2"> etc., <gloss>win back the lost repute.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possetis_2">, purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="depeculatorem_2">etc.: for a more complete statement of these charges, see chs. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.4">iv</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.5">v</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="juris_urbani_2"> i.e. <gloss>as praetor urbanus</gloss> (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.12">12</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religiose_3"> <gloss>according to your oath.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religionem_veritatemque_3"> here, <gloss>feeling of obligation and regard for the truth.</gloss>Notice that the Latin, having a comparatively poor vocabulary, is obliged to use one word for all the phrases or sides of an idea; hence such a word as <foreign xml:lang="lat">veritas</foreign> may mean <gloss>truth</gloss> (abstractly), <gloss>a truth</gloss> (concretely), the <gloss>truth</gloss> (generally), <gloss>regard for truth</gloss>, or <gloss>truthful conduct.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicium_3">, etc., i.e. the <gloss>court</gloss> will be found wanting,—not a suitable defendant or a zealous prosecutor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equidem_3">, i.e. for my own part. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quas_partim_3"> <gloss>some of which.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="devitarim_3">subj. as a part of the concession contained in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_sint_3">: § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324, I; G. 663, I; H. 652 (529, ii); H-B. 539. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_neque_3">, following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="numquam_3"> does not destroy the negative, but is more emphatic than <foreign xml:lang="lat">aut . . . aut</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istius_4">: see note on Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl4.16">p. 4, l. 16</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Glabrioni_4">: the praetor presiding. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ordini_senatorio_4"> <gloss>the senatorial order, nay, the very name of Senator.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictitat_4"> <gloss>constantly repeats:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 263">263</ref>, 2 (167, b); B. 155, 2, a; G. 191, I; H. 364 (336); H.-B. 212, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_metuendum_4">: for <foreign xml:lang="lat">erat met.</foreign> in dir. disc.; hence followed by the secondary sequence, i.e. <gloss>those would have to fear</gloss> (if the case were theirs), but <gloss> he,</gloss> etc. § <ref n="AG 584">584</ref>, a and N. (336 A, N.l); H.-B. 581, b, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_4">, i.e. only what. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multis_4">i.e. not only for himself but also for his counsel and for those whom he may wish to bribe,—in particular, the jurors (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.40">40</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pecunia_4">belongs to both clauses, as is shown by their parallelism. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_4">for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c (287, c); B. 268, 7; G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); cf. H.-B. 478. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_5">imperf. subj. in protasis of a <gloss>continued</gloss> condition lasting till now; § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, a (308, a); B. 304, 2; G. 597, R.l; H. 579, I (510, N.2); H.-B. 581. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fefellisset_5"> <gloss>he would have eluded us.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cadit_5">pres. tense, of an action lasting till now; § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 533 (467, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="corrumpendi_judici_5"> , <gloss>of bribing the court</gloss> (cf. our phrase <q rend="double">bribery and corruption</q>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factus_sit_5">for sequence, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c, N.2 (287, C, N.); B. 268, 6; G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); cf. H.-B. 478; notice that the perf. would necessarily be used in the dir. disc. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_primum_5"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempus_offenderet_5"> <gloss>he hit an unfavorable time;</gloss> because popular sentiment was already so exasperated in regard to the corruption of the courts. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_inquirendi_6"> i.e. <gloss>for going into Sicily to make an investigation</gloss> (hence the acc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invenit_qui_6"> <gloss>he found some one who.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Achaiam_6">, sc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inquirendi_6">on this trumped-up case, which was intended to have the precedence of the trial of Verres, see Introd. to this Oration (p. 27). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_conficeret_6">purp. clause dependent on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invenit_6"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Brundisium_6"> <gloss> Brindisi</gloss>,the port whence the greater part of Italian travel, now as then, embarks for the East. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obii_6"> ,<gloss>went throughout</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populorum_6"> ,<gloss>commmunities:</gloss> the word <foreign xml:lang="lat"> populus, </foreign>meaning originally <gloss>multitude</gloss>, is a semi-abstract noun often used to denote the community in its official capacity Our use of the word <gloss>people</gloss> in some later meanings frequently produces confusion in the minds of beginners. The political system of the ancients was composed of an indefinite number of petty communities, all possessing a certain degree of independence. Hence the plur. is used here to indicate several such communities. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_posset_6"> (clause of result), imperf by seq. of tenses: translate, however, <gloss> can.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_obsideret_6"> (purpose), <gloss>to block my chance</gloss> (of bringing Verres to trial). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_7">i.e. now that his former scheme has failed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_7"> ,<gloss>this new idea.</gloss> What the idea is is detailed in sects. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.7">7</ref>,<ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.8">8</ref>: viz. the reasons for desiring a postponement together with grounds for hoping for it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_7"> ,<gloss> citizens</gloss>, i.e. Romans travelling or doing business in the provinces, or provincials who had received the citizenship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socios_7"> <gloss> allies:</gloss> citizens of communities which, although embraced within the boundaries of Roman provinces, had, for special reasons, been allowed to retain a nominal independence, with their own laws and magistrates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatibus_7"> <gloss> documents</gloss>, i.e. official testimony (<q rend="double"> resolutions,</q> etc.) relating to the acts of Verres. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonis_8"> ,<gloss>good citizens:</gloss> here, as generally in Cicero, used in a partisan sense for the aristocracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="experiatur_8">this violates the sequence of tenses in order to make the meaning clear; the imperf. would refer to the time of getting the money, not to the present moment; cf. § <ref n="AG 481">481</ref>, N. (287, h, N.); B. 268, 7; G. 509, 1, N.; H.-B. 478. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_8">: the subj. shows that this is the thought of Verres, and not merely something thrown in by Cicero. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempus_8">: the present scheme of the defence is by corrupt means to stave off the trial to a more advantageous time (see chs. vi-viii). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posses_8">imperf. to express his purpose at the time of the purchase. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="criminum_vim_8"> ,<gloss>the force of the charges.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poterat_8">:indic., the reason being Cicero’s. (The whole passage is an instructive example of the freedom of a living language from its own trammels. Rules are made for language, not language for rules.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eloquentia_gratia_9">even mere rhetorical skill or personal influence would be, to a criminal who had no case </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causa_9"> a respectable (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honesto_9">) means of escape compared with these attempts at corruption. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profecto_9"> ,<gloss>I am sure</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aucuparetur_9"> <gloss>be fishing for</gloss> (lit. <gloss>set nets for birds</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_fieret_9"> <gloss>as to have some one chosen to be put on trial</gloss> ; the Senate itself was insulted by the selection of one of its members to be set up as a man of straw, that Verres might get clear. The reference is to the trumped-up case with regard to abuses in Achaia (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.6">6</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_9">, i.e. Verres. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causam_diceret_9"> <gloss>stand trial</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_rebus_10"> ,<gloss>from this</gloss> (abl. of means with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perspicio_10"> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilio_10"> ,<gloss>panel</gloss>, i.e. the body of jurors (cf. Rosc. Am., p. 24, I. 2). An obvious, and apparently a deserved, compliment. Whatever the general character of the courts, Cicero had in this instance secured a jury on whom he could rely. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_judicavit_10"> <gloss>decided at the challenging</gloss> (<q rend="double">throwing out</q>) <gloss>of the jury</gloss>, i.e. on seeing the kind of men challenged by the two sides respectively. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_arbitraretur_10">subst. clauses of result (justified by the introductory </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ea_10">) instead of the more regular acc. and inf. of ind. disc.; § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, c (332,f); <gloss>G. 557</gloss>, R. and N1; cf. H. 571,4 (501, iii). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_10">introducing the reason of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullam_rem_10">, etc., above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adulescentiae_11">i.e. before he entered public life. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestura_11"> <gloss> quaestorship</gloss>, the first grade of political honor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Carbonem_11">Carbo was the leader of the Marian faction after the death of Marius and Cinna. He was consul <date when="-0082">B.C. 82</date>, the year of Sulla’s return and victory. Verres was his quaestor (or paymaster), and went over to the enemy with the money-chest when he saw which side was likely to prevail. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessitudinem_religionemque_11">the quaestor was originally nominated specially by the consul; and the peculiarly close and sacred relation (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> necessitudo</foreign>) existing between them was known as <foreign xml:lang="lat">pietas</foreign>,—a sentiment akin to filial affection. The designation by lot (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sors</foreign>) was also held to be a token of divine will, and therefore sacred (<foreign xml:lang="lat">religio</foreign>). In betraying his consul, then, Verres was guilty of more than an ordinary breach of trust,—he committed an act of impiety. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legatio_11">Verres was in <date from="-0080" to="-0079"> <date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date>-79</date> <foreign xml:lang="lat">legatus</foreign> and acting quaestor (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_quaestore_11">) of Dolabella, whose province was Cilicia. The extortions of the two were practiced in the adjoining regions of Pamphylia, Pisidia, and parts of Asia (i.e. of the Roman province of Asia, the old kingdom of Pergamus, embracing the western part of Asia Minor); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="totius_11"> is a rhetorical exaggeration. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scelus_quaestorium_11">Verres treated Dolabella much as he had treated Carbo. Neither of these infamous commanders deserved better treatment; but this does not excuse the perfidy of Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pro_quaestore_11_2"> <gloss>acting quaestor:</gloss>when there was a vacancy in a provincial quaestorship, the commander might appoint any person to perform the duties of the office. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adduxit_11">Dolabella, in addition to the odium of his own crimes, had to bear the infamy of the outrageous acts of Verres; and after all Verres saved himself by turning against him (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oppugnavit_11">) and appearing as a witness in his trial for extortion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aedium_12">etc. The public buildings were regularly under the charge of the aedile, not of the praetor; the cases referred to here were certain flagrant instances of corruption and extortion arising out of contracts for public buildings, in which the praetor had it exceptionally in his power to interfere for his own advantage. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_dicere_12"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">jurisdictio</foreign>),<gloss>declaring the law</gloss>, was the primary function of the praetor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonorum_addictio_12">is the adjudging of property to a claimant; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condonatio_12"> (<gloss> grant</gloss>) is the act of giving it up to a defendant: no matter which way the decision of Verres went in a case, his action was sure to be unlawful and for his own corrupt ends. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="instituta_12"> ,<gloss> precedents</gloss>. The edicts of the praetors made up a body of common law, not absolutely binding, however, on their successors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cursare_25"> (historical infinitive), <gloss>ran hither and thither.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appellare_convenire_25"> <gloss>accosted and conferred with.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cuius_25">: the antecedent is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eiusdem_25"> (l.19). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_fide_25">, i.e. <gloss>his good faith</gloss> to the Sicilians: probably a hint that Cicero himself had been approached with a bribe. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eo_26"> ,<gloss>in this course</gloss>, i.e. postponing the trial. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_26">indir. disc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praerogativam_26"> ,<gloss>an earnest</gloss>. In the <gloss>comitia centuriata</gloss>, it was determined by lot which century should first cast its vote. The vote of this century, called <gloss> praerogativa</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">praerogo</foreign>), was superstitiously regarded as an omen or <gloss>earnest</gloss> of the result which it was likely to decide. Hence the word is here used of the effective support given to Metellus at the polls by Verres. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">praerogativa</foreign> which Q. Metellus gave to Verres, in return for the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> praerogativae</foreign> of the comitia, is described in the next section. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cuiquam_27"> ,<gloss>for anything.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alter_designatus_27">: Q. Caecilius Metellus Creticus (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.21"> 21</ref>). The three brothers, fast friends of Verres, were so situated as to promise the greatest help the next year, when Quintus would be consul, and Marcus praetor, presiding over the court of <foreign xml:lang="lat">Repetundae</foreign>, while Lucius was already pro-praetor in Sicily. Some of the Sicilians, therefore, obeyed the summons of Metellus, although they had disregarded that of Hortensius (sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.25">25</ref>). The object of Metellus was to induce the Sicilians to withdraw the suit, or at any rate to refrain from appearing as witnesses. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaesiturum_27"> (technical term), <gloss>was to preside over the court.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_faceres_28">apodosis of cont. to fact construction, with protasis implied in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="innocente_28"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alienissimum_28"> ,<gloss>no kin whatever of yours.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictitat_28"> <gloss>says incessantly</gloss> (see next section). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alicui_28">depends upon</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videatur_28"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignoret_28">subj. of characteristic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fato_ceteros_29">etc.: the Metelli seemed born to hold office. Cicero here alludes to a verse written by the poet Naevius, a hundred and fifty years before: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> <q rend="double">Fato Metelli Romae fiunt consules.</q> </foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populi_existimationi_29"> <gloss>reputation with the people.—</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Glabrionem_29">observe the skill with which this compliment to the praetor before whom Cicero is now arguing the case, and the following compliments to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">judices</foreign>, are put into the mouth of Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_29">: referring to what follows. Cicero makes Verres point out the changes in the jury which must follow from changes in the government that is to come in with the new year. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlega_29">: both Caesonius and Cicero were <foreign xml:lang="lat">aediles designati</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="expediat_29">: fut. apodosis with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conemur_29">as its</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_29">protasis, but hardly to be distinguished from subj. of characteristic; cf. § <ref n="AG 534">534</ref> (319, headnote). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Juniano_consilio_29"> referring to a case four years before, in which wholesale bribery had been proved, so that the presiding praetor, Junius, as well as almost the entire <foreign xml:lang="lat">consilium</foreign> (body of jurors), had been stamped with infamy. Caesonius, a member of the jury, had been proof against corruption, and had disclosed the whole affair (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_protulit_29"> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hunc_judicem_29"> <gloss>him as juryman.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_Jan_29">after the New Year; for at that time he would be excluded from the panel by his aedileship. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Sulpicius_30">: he had probably just been elected quaestor. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Non_Dec_30">. (Dec. 5): on this day the new quaestors entered on their office. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="L_Cassius_30">: the family characteristic here stated was proverbial (<foreign xml:lang="lat">Cassiani judices</foreign>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribuni_militares_30">at this time legion-commanders. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_judicabunt_30"> ,<gloss>will not serve as jurors.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subsortiemur_30">i.e. we shall draw another <gloss>to fill his place.</gloss> This is the regular use of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sub_30"> in similar compounds: as <gloss>suffectus, subrogatus</gloss> etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prope_toto_30">: the jury, therefore, apparently consisted of about twelve or fifteen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Nonae_31">, etc.: it was, therefore, about 3 P.M. of the 5th of August. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="votivos_31">: these games were in celebration of Pompey’s victory over the Marian party in Spain (<date when="-0072">B.C. 72</date>). The votive games would occupy from Aug. 16 to Sept. I (August had at this time only 29 days); On Sept. 4 began the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Ludi Romani</foreign>, continuing till the 19th. The intervening days (Sept. 2, 3) were of no account for the trial, so that it could not be resumed before Sept. 20, a suspension of 34 days (<foreign xml:lang="lat">prope quadraginta</foreign>). The <foreign xml:lang="lat">Ludi Victoriae</foreign> (established by Sulla in honor of his victory) would continue from Oct. 27 to Nov. I, and the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Ludi Plebeii</foreign>, from Nov. 4 to Nov. 17. All these games were sacred festivals, during which business was suspended: the time was occupied with religious observances, accompanied by races and dramatic entertainments. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tum_denique_31"> ,<gloss>not till then.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Victoriae_31">see Fig. 4 (from the Column of Trajan). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perpauci_31">for the month of December was full of festivals. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_integram_31">i.e. from the beginning. The zeal of the prosecution would have flagged, the public interest would have cooled down, and the jury would be almost wholly new. The case would therefore have to be taken up <foreign xml:lang="lat">de novo</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_retinuissem_31">, i.e. I should have challenged him. Metellus was now one of the jurors. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_32">opposed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_essem_32"> above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eo_32">, etc. (abl. abs.), <gloss>with him as juryman.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jurato_32"> <gloss>on oath.</gloss> The <foreign xml:lang="lat">judices</foreign> were sworn; the praetor was not. Metellus might therefore be trusted to vote honestly as a juror, though he might, when praetor, connive at the corruption of the jury men. Cicero ran less risk of offending Metellus by thus accusing him of extreme partisanship than if he had accused him of perjury. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legitimo_tempore_32">: he had a right to use twenty days for developing the points of the prosecution. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="capiam_32">i.e. by showing, in a long speech, how carefully he had prepared his case. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_elabatur_32">, with</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="periculum_est_32">which takes the constr. of a verb of fearing. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_32">see § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>, a (320, a); B. 283, 2; G. 631, 2; H. 591 (503, i); H.-B 521, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perpetua_oratione_33"> <gloss>a continuous argument</gloss>, before bringing up the witnesses. This is what we possess in the five speeches of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Accusatio</foreign>, which, in the usual order of proceeding, would have been delivered before bringing up the witnesses, but which were in fact never spoken at all (see Introd. to the oration, page 28). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="percipi_33"> <gloss> reaped:</gloss> the regular term for gathering crops. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potuit_33"> ,<gloss>might have been:</gloss> §<ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, c (308, c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 597, R.3; H. 583 (511, 1, N.3); H.-B. 582, 3,a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="publicis_33">see note on <ref n="pl35.23">p. 35, l. 23</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabulis_33"> <gloss>records.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatibus_33"> ,<gloss> documents.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_omnis_33">here, after stating his plan briefly, Cicero goes off into a seeming digression against Hortensius. In this he shows clearly one of his principal motives in undertaking the prosecution, namely, to overthrow the latter’s excessive control of the courts. The attack is skillfully introduced. His sole reason, he says, for departing from the ordinary course of procedure is that Hortensius does not wish to meet him in fair legal fight. The sally against Hortensius, again, serves as a transition to Cicero’s final appeal to the sense of shame and the prudence of the court. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diluendis_explicandis_33">technical terms in argument (see Vocab.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_nature_33">Hortensius, like M. Metullus, was personally an amiable and honorable man, though pledged to a bad cause. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rationi_33"> <gloss>scheme, course</gloss>, looking to the method; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilio_33"> <gloss>plan of action</gloss>, looking to the end. Cicero contrasts them more than once. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="binos_ludos_34">i.e. Pompey’s and the Roman games. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comperendinem_34"> <gloss>close my case</gloss> (lit. <gloss>adjourn over</gloss>). After the testimony was all in, it was customary to adjourn over to the next day but one (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comperendinare_34">), in order to give opportunity for a rehearing (usually a brief one). When this stage had been reached, there was no chance for further postponement. Cicero’s determination to bring about a <foreign xml:lang="lat">comperendinatio</foreign> before Pompey’s games—i.e. within ten days settled the case in his favor; for, as has been shown, the only hope of the defence lay in putting off the trial, Hortensius having absolutely nothing to say in behalf of his client’s innocence. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessarium_34"> ,<gloss> unavoidable</gloss> (not a mere shrewd trick like that of Hortensius). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_34">: refers forward to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eos_velle_34">, etc. (l. 24). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplum_praeclarum_34"> <gloss>an honor and distinction</gloss> (translating as nouns). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="innocentiae_34"> (an almost technical term), <gloss>purity of administration</gloss> in Sicily (see Introd. to the oration). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="majus_quiddam_34">: what this was is explained in sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.35">35</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_35">: refers to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istum_vocari_35"> (l. 29). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potentia_35"> <gloss> domineering</gloss>(i.e. his illegal control of the courts); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupiditas_35"> (in a bad sense), <gloss>unscrupulous eagerness</gloss> (for gaining your case). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interponeretur_35">for fut. ind. of the dir. disc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_35">opposed to the time of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videbatur_35"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regnum_judiciorum_35"> <gloss>lording it over the courts.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="homines_35">i.e. the corrupt senatorial jurors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inruere_35">, etc., <gloss>to be bent on making themselves hateful and offensive.</gloss> —hoc, i.e. to break down Hortensius’s control, and the corruption of a few Senators. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nervos_aetatis_35">Cicero was now 36. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ordo_36">, i.e. the Senate. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="paucorum_36">, artfully put so as not to offend the whole body. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="loco_36"> :<gloss>the Rostra</gloss> (see Vocab., under <foreign xml:lang="lat">rostrum</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="secum_agere_36">the technical expression for transacting business in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> was <foreign xml:lang="lat">agere cum populo</foreign> (or <foreign xml:lang="lat">plebe</foreign>). Cicero refers to the office of curule aedile, upon which he was to enter January 1. One of the most important functions of this magistrate was the administration of criminal justice (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_improbis_36">) in cases where there had been an appeal from the sentence of a court to the judgment of the public assembly. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="munus_36"> <gloss> service.</gloss> The word also means the <gloss>public games</gloss>, which were given to the people by the aediles especially; hence there is a kind of pun here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="moneo_36">, etc.: observe the climax. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deponere_36"> ,<gloss> deposit</gloss> with the <foreign xml:lang="lat">sequestres</foreign> (see note on <ref n="pl36.15">p. 36, l. 15</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accipere_36"> ,<gloss> take</gloss> (money); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recipere_36"> <gloss> undertake</gloss> to do anything (upon request or the like). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="polliceri_36"> ,<gloss> offer.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interpretes_36"> <gloss> go-betweens:</gloss> the <gloss>divisores</gloss> are probably meant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potentiam_36">: it is hardly accidental that this is the same word used above (sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.35">35</ref>, l. 31) of the influence of Hortensius. In the next section Cicero expressly asserts that he expects to meet with all possible opposition from the latter. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erit_37"> <gloss>will be</gloss> (it is true): notice the</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_37">emphatic position, opposing it to the clause with</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_37"> (l. 24). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperio_potestate_37"> <gloss>military and civil power.</gloss> Of the regular magistrates, all possessed <foreign xml:lang="lat">potestas</foreign>, i.e. power in general (including military power); but only consuls and praetors possessed the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium,</foreign>—i.e. sovereign power, as of a general in the field, somewhat limited, however, in the city by special privileges of Roman citizens. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commemorabuntur_37"> <gloss>shall be mentioned</gloss> (by me). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certis_rebus_37"> <gloss>well-ascertained facts.</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agentur_37"> <gloss>made ground of action.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inter_annos_37">i.e since Sulla’s <foreign xml:lang="lat">lex judiciaria</foreign>, transferring the courts to the senatorial order (see note on Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl2.1">p. 2, l. 1</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quinquaginta_38">i.e. from the law of Caius Gracchus, <date when="-0123">B.C. 123</date>, to that of Sulla, <date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_suspicio_38">one of the exaggerations of the advocate. If the courts were really worse in <date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date> than they had been in 90, it was simply because the times were worse. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sublata_38"> <gloss>taken away.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populi_Romani_38">etc., i.e. the ability of the people to hold in check the senatorial order by means of the tribunician power suspended by Sulla (see note on <ref n="pl43.32">p. 43, l. 32</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Q_Calidius_38"> praetor <date when="-0079">B.C. 79</date>; condemned for extortion in Spain. It seems that Calidius, being condemned <foreign xml:lang="lat">de repetundis</foreign>, with bitter irony assailed the bribed jurors on account of the smallness of the bribe for which he was condemned, saying that it was not respectable (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honestum_38">) to condemn an ex-praetor for so small a sum. The allusion shows that the corruption was notorious and universal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="HS_triciens_38"> <gloss>3,000,000 sestertii</gloss> = $150,000 (nearly); § <ref n="AG 634">634</ref> (379); G. p. 493; H. 757 (647, iv, I); H.-B. 675, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praetorium_38">an ex-magistrate kept the rank of the highest office he had held,—as <gloss>consularis, praetorius, aedilicius.</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="P_Septimio_38"> (Scaevola), condemned <date when="-0072">B.C. 72</date>; the damages were increased because of his connection with the odious <foreign xml:lang="lat">consilium Junianum</foreign> (sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.29">29</ref>).
